text: FORZA (LA), a small fortified town of Sicily, in the Val-di-Demona, at the foot of a hill of the same name, 3 m. S of Messina, near Point Santo Alessio.
FOS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, 60 m. SW of Toulouse. Pop. 1,603.
FOSCALDO, a town of Naples, in Calabria-Citra, 16 m. NW of Cosenza. Pop. 2,000.
FOSDI-NOVO, a small town of Modena, 8 m. NW of Carrara. Pop. 1,448.

FOSDYKE, a parish of Lincolnshire, 6 m. NNW of Holbeach. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. in 1831, 401; in 1841, 601. An embankment and bridge are here carried across an arm of the sea, called Fosdykewash, 1 m. in width, whereby two parts of Lincolnshire are connected which before were at a distance of 16 m., unless the passenger crossed a dangerous ferry. The bank is erected across a tract of sand, and made of the same material; but worked in such a manner as to receive the beat of the sea without injury.
FOSENO, a small island in the North sea, near the coast of Norway, in N lat. 60° 45'.
FOSS, or Fosse, a river of England, which runs into the Ouse near York.
FOSSACECA, a village of Naples in Abruzzo- Citra, 4 m. E of Lanciano.—Also a v. in the prov. of Sannio, 8 m. N of Campobasso.

FOSSANO, a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. of Coni, situated on a rising ground on the l. bank of the Stura, and on a canal which unites that river with the Po, 12 m. NE of Coni. Its name is a corruption of Fons sanus, - an epithet derived from its mineral springs. It has been a bishop's see since 1592; and contained, with the adjacent territory, a pop. of 16,041 in 1839, who are partly engaged in the manufacture of silk fabrics, paper, and leather. This town was taken by Bonaparte in the outset of his career, in April 1796; in November 1799 it fell into the hands of the Austrians; but was lost once more in June 1800, after the battle of Marengo.
FOSSA-NUOVA, a village of the Papal states, situated on the ruins of the ancient Appli Forum, 2 m. Sof Piperno. It has a Cistercian abbey, in which the celebrated Thomas Aquinas died.
FOSSA-PAITANA, a small river of Lombardy, in the Paduan; it falls into the gulf of Venice.
FOSSAT (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ariège, on the I. bank of the Leze, 21 m. W of Mirepoix. Pop. of cant., comprising 11 com., 12,390; of com., 915.
FOSSATO, a town of the Papal states, in the marquisate of Ancona, among the Apennines, 7 m. SW of Fabriano. Pop. 1,616.

FOSSAWAY AND TULLIEBOLE, two united parishes, the former belonging to the co. of Perth, and the other annexed to Kinross. They occupy a considerable extent of the Ochil hills, and the valley beneath, from the river Devon to within 6 m. of Kinross. Pop. 1,724 in 1841.

FOSSE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Marigny. - Also a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, cant. of Stavelot. Pop. 107.—Also a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxemburg, cant. of Grand Menie. Pop. 155.
FOSSE, or Fosses, a canton and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, between the Sambre and the Meuse, 7 m. SW of Namur. Pop. 2,378.
FOSSE' (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Forges. Pop. 540.
FOSSEMAGNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne.

FOSSE-AU-MORTIER, a small lake of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, 11 m. from Mezieres. The sides consist of an argillaceous and slippery earth; the bottom has never been reached by the soundingline, and it is supposed to have been the crater of a volcano long since extinct.
FOSSE'S (LES), a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxemburg, cant. of Assenois. Pop. 230.

FOSSOMBRONE, a town of the Papal states, in the duchy and 10 m. ESE of Urbino, on the l. bank of the Metauro, which is here crossed by a fine bridge. Pop. in 1833, 6,421.—F. is the ancient Forum Sempronii; and in its neighbourhood Asdrubal, the brother of Hannibal, was defeated and killed. It was destroyed in after ages by the Goths and Lombards. It is the see of a bishop.
FOSSUM, a town of Norway, in the prov. of Aggerhuus, bail. of Mandal, 2 m. N of Skeen. There are extensive iron-works in the vicinity.
FOSTAT. See CAIRO.
FOSTER, a township of Rhode Island, U. S., 19 m. WSW of Providence. Pop. 2,181.

FOSTON, a parish in Lincolnshire, 6 m. NNW of Grantham. Area 2,180 acres. Pop. in 1831, 441; in 1841, 497.—Also a p. in the co., and 6 m. SSE of Leicester. Area 840 acres. Pop. in 1831, 32; in 1841, 41.—Also a p. in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 12 m. NE of York. Area 2,090 acres. Pop. in 1831, 283; in 1841, 370.—Also a p. in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. ESE of Great-Driffield. Area 4,560 acres. Pop. in 1831, 715; in 1841, 792.
FOTHERBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 3 m. NNW of Louth. Area 1,400 acres. Pop. in 1831, 207; 1,139. in 1841, 227.

FOTHERINGAY, a village and parish of England, in the co. of Northampton, 3 m. NE of Oundle, situated on the river Nen. Area of p., 2,110 acres. dep. of Sarthe, 11 m. N by E of La Fleche. Pop. 660. III. * FOU Pop. in 1831, 280; in 1841, 230. Here are the remains of an ancient castle, wherein Mary, queen of Scots, was beheaded in 1586.
FOTIPET, a town of Hindostan, in Bednore, 5 m. ENE of Bednore.
FOU. See Fu.

FOUAH, a village of Lower Egypt, on the r. bank of the W arm of the Nile, opposite Atfieh. The late pasha established several spinning-jennies and carding-machines here for the manufacture of caps in imitation of those of Fez in Tunis.
FOU-AN, a town of the kingdom of Corea, 35 m. WNW of Coufu.
FOUCARMONT, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, 9 m. N by W of Neufchatel-en-Bray. Pop. 631.

FOUCAULT, or CALDWELL MANOR, a seignory of Lower Canada, in the co. of Bonville, bounded on the E by Missiskoui bay, and containing an area 6 m. broad in front, and about 7½ m. in depth, watered by Wolf creek, an affluent of South river, and on the W by the Richelieu. The line of boundary between Canada and the United States penetrates it towards the S. It is generally level, and possesses considerable fertility. The timber is of good quality.
FOUCHENGE, or Foushenge, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Khorassan, 25 m. N of Herat. FOUCHERANS; a commune of France, in the dep. of Jura, cant. of Dole. Pop. 469.
FOUESNANT, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Finistere, cant. and 8 m. SE of Quimper. Pop. 3,172.
FOUG, a small town of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, in Lorraine, 4 m. W of Toul. Pop. 1,402.
FOUGARON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garon, cant. of Aspet. Pop. 1,077.
FOUGAX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ariege, cant. of Lavelanet, on the l. bank of the Frau. Pop. 1,756.
FOUGERAIS (LE), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Les Essarts. Pop. 604.

FOUGERAY, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, arrond. of Redon .- The cant. comprises 2 com. Pop. in 1841, 5,862.— The com., 19 m. ENE of Redon, had a pop. of 5,254 in 1841.
FOUGERE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine - et - Loire, cant. and 5 m. N of Baugé. Pop. 1,596.

FOUGE'RES, an arrondissement, canton, and commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine. -The arrond., comprising the 5 cant. of Antrain, Saint - Aubin - du - Cormier, Saint - Brice - en - Coglais, F., and Louvigne-du-Desert, had a pop. of 81,676 in 1841. Area 99,639 hect.—The cant., comprising 18 com., had a pop. of 28,409 in 1841.—The com. and town are situated on the l. bank of the Nanson, 32 m. NE of Rennes. Pop. in 1836, 9,384; in 1841, 9,182. It has manufactures of shoes, leather, paper, and coarse linens.
FOUGERETS (Les), a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Carentoir. Pop. 1,105.
FOUGEREUSE (LA), a small town of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, 3 m. W of Argentau-le- Chateau.
FOUGEROLLES, a town of France, in the dep. and 17 m. NW of Mayenne. Pop. 5,591.
FOUGEROLLES L'EGLISE, a village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, 6 m. N of Luxeuil. Pop.
FOUILLADE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Najac. Pop. 1,593.
FOUILLETOURTE, a small town of France,
FOUILLOUSE (LA), a small town of France, in the dep. of Loire, cant. of Saint-Heant, 11 m. SE of Montbrison. Pop. 3,471.
FOUIN, a territory in Western Africa, to the N of Ardrah.
FOU-KIANG. See Fu-KIANG.

FOUL BAY, a bay on the S coast of the island of Java, in S lat. 8° 38'.—Also a bay on the NE coast of the island of Barbadoes.—Also a bay on the S coast, and near the E extremity of the island of Jamaica.
FOUL ISLAND, a small island in the bay of Bengal, on the coast of Aracan, in N lat. 18° 12'.— Also an island in the Chinese sea, near the coast of Cochin-China, in N lat. 11° 73'.
FOUL ISLANDS, a cluster of small islands in the Eastern sea, near the N coast of the island of Flores, in S lat. 8° 9'.

FOUL POINT, a cape on the N coast of New Guinea, in S lat. 0° 46'.—Also the outer SE point of Trincomalee inlet on the coast of Ceylon, bearing SE ½ E 5½ m. from Flagstaff-point.—Also a red cliffy head on the N coast of Australia.

FOULA, or Fulla, one of the Shetland islands, 16 m. W of the mainland. It is about 3 m. in length. and half as much in breadth, with bold and steep sides towards the W. There is a landing-place on the E side, but it has no harbour. Pop. in 1841, 202. This island is by some authors supposed to be the Ultima Thule of the ancients.
FOULAHS. See FELLATAHS.
FOULAIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, cant. of Nogent-le-Roi. Pop. 399.
FOULAYRONNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Agen. Pop. 1,231.

FOULDEN, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. ESE of Stoke-Ferry. Area 3,500 acres. Pop. in 1831, 500; in 1841, 500.—Also a p. in the co. and 5 m. WNW of Berwick. Area 3,000 acres. Pop. in 1831, 424; in 1841, 393.

FOULE POINT, or MAROFOTOTRA, a headland on the coast of Madagascar, in S lat. 17° 40', E long. 50°. A small port here is the most frequented of any in the NE part of the island. The shores are very bold, and the least depth of water at low tide is 23 ft.; but the rise or fall never exceeds from 4 to 5 ft. Several rivers flow into the sea in the vicinity. They abound in fish, wild fowl, and alligators. A number of Arabs, as well as French traders, from Mauritius and Bourbon, have settlements here.
FOULIS (WESTER), a parish and village of Perthshire, on the Almond. Pop. of p. in 1831, 1,238; in 1841, 1,171, of whom 187 were in the v.
FOULMIRE, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 6 m. NE of Royston. Area 2,302 acres. Pop. in 1831, 547; in 1841, 610.
FOULNESS, an island of England, on the coast of Essex, 9 m. ENE of Rochford, celebrated for the oysters taken at the mouth of the Crouch. Area 8,060 acres. Pop. in 1831, 630; in 1841, 674.
FOULOU-SOUSOU, a town of Chinese Tartary, on the river Amur, 9 m. NW of Telden.
FOULRIDGE, a township in the p. of Whalley, in Lancashire, 2 m. N of Colne. Pop. 1,458.
FOULSHAM, a parish in Norfolk, 11 m. N of Dereham. Pop. in 1831, 958; in 1841, 1,048. The village was almost totally destroyed by fire in 1770.
FOULSTON, a township in the p. of Kirk-Burton, 6 m. SSE of Huddersfield. Pop. in 1831, 1,573; in 1841, 1,856.

FOULWEATHER (CAPE), a conspicuous promontory on the NW coast of N. America, of a very FOU jects abruptly into the sea; a remarkable table-hill is situated on the N, and a lower round bluff point to the S of it. Vancouver places this cape in N lat. 44° 49′, W long. 125° 56′.
FOULWIND (CAPE), a promontory of New Zealand, on the NW side of New Ulster, in S lat. 41° 46′, and E long. 171° 31′.

FOUNAI, or Fu-NEI, an important commercial town of Japan, in the island of Kiusiu, prov. of Boungo, 138 m. ENE of Nagasaki, and 81 m. SE of Kokoura, on a river which throws itself into the Boungo channel.

FOUNTAIN, a county in the state of Indiana, U. S., comprising an area of 390 sq. m., bordered on the W by Wabash river, and watered by its affluents, Coal creek, Shawnee creeks, and intersected by the Wabash and Erie canal. Its surface is undulating, and its soil extremely fertile. Pop. in 1840, 11,218.

FOUNTAINS-EARTH, a township in the p. of Kirkby-Malzeard, W. R. of Yorkshire, 31 m. SW of Ripon. Area 6,840 acres. Pop. 435. Near this township, in Studley Park, in a beautiful valley, tra versed by the rivulet Skell, is Fountain's abbey, the finest and most venerable monastic ruin in Britain. It was founded in 1204 by John de Ebor, and is said to have covered an area of 12 acres.
FOUQUEBRUNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. of La Vallette. Pop. 1,124. It affords good wine.
FOUQUET, a small river of Lower Canada, in the seignories of Grandville and Lachenaye, which flows into the Riviere-des-Caps.
FOUQUEURE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. of Aigre. Pop. 1,045.
FOUQUEVILLERS, a town of France, in the dep. of the Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Pas, 13 m. SSW of Arras. Pop. 1,900. It has several oil manufactories.
FOURAKA, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Dewa, 21 m. NE of Nigata, on the sea of Japan.

FOURAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente-Inferieure, cant. and 9 m. NW of Rochefort, on the r. bank of the embouchure of the Charente. Pop. 860. It has a good port, and carries on a considerable trade.
FOURBISSART, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. of Lessines. Pop. 290.
FOUR-BROTHERS, a group of small islands in the Java sea, to the E of the island of Kangelang, in S lat. 7° 5′, and E long. 115°.
FOURCE'S, a town of France, in the dep. of the Gers, cant. and 4 m. NNE of Montreal, on the Lauson. Pop. 1,102. Turquoises of fine quality are found in the environs.
FOURCHAMBAULT, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Nièvre, cant. of Pougues, and com. of Garchizy. Pop. 700. It has extensive ironworks, which give employment to about 3,000 hands.

FOURCHE (BAYOU LA), a river of the state of Louisiana, U. S., one of the numerous arms into which the Mississippi divides itself in the lower part of its course. It separates from the Mississippi near Donaldsonville, in the parish of Ascension; runs through that of Assomption; forms the boundary line between the parishes of Terre-Bonne and of La Fourche or Lafourche Interieure; and after a SE course of about 108 m., falls into the gulf of Mexico, to the E of the bay of Timballier.
FOURCHE (GRANDE), a river of Lower Canada, which runs through the Cote-de-la-Grande-Fourche, across the Temisconata portage into the NW branch of Trois Pistoles river.

FOURCHE (Petite), a river of Lower Canada, singular appearance. A high round bluff point pro- | which issues from the lake Des Sangues, crosses the Temisconata Portage, and flows into the SW branch | of the Trois Pistoles river.
FOURCROIX (LE), a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, dep. of Blandain. Pop. 405.
FOUR-FACARDINS, a group of small islands, 4 in number, in the Low archipelago, S. Pacific, in S lat. 18° 40′, and W long. 138° 50′.
FOURGS (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Doubs, cant. and 6 m. SSE of Pontarlier. Pop. 1,275.
FOURMALOT, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, dep. of Momignies. Pop. 224.

FOURMIES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Trelon, on the l. bank of the Petite-Helpe, 10 m. SSE of Avesnes. Pop. in 1841, 3,634. It has manufactories of wood-ware, glass, and thread, cotton spinning and wool-carding-mills, a bleachery, and several blast furnaces.
FOUR-MILE-BURN, a hamlet in the p. of Donegore, co. Antrim. Pop. in 1831, 73.
FOUR-MILE-WATER, a rivulet in co. Cork, which runs SW to the head of Dunmanus bay.- Also a village in the p. of Kilronan, co. Waterford, on the Nier, 61 m. SW of Clonmel.

FOURNEAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire, cant. of Saint-Symphorien-de- Lay, 13 m. ESE of Roanne. Pop. 1,170.—Also a village of Sardinia, in the div. of Savoy, prov. of Maurienne, 19 m. ESE of Saint-Jean-de-Maurienne, on the l. bank of the Arc. Pop. 160. It has lead and copper mines and founderies.—Also a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, dep. of Marchin. Pop. 240.

FOURNELS, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Lozère, arrond. of Marvejols. The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 4,381; in 1841, 4,819. The village is 24 m. NNW of Marvejols. Pop. 310. Caddis is manufactured here.
FOURNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of La Bassée, 9 m. WSW of Lille. Pop. 1,385. Sugar from beet-root is extensively manufactured here.
FOURNET, a village of Sardinia, in the div. of Savoy, prov. of Tarentaise, 25 m. ESE of Moutiers, near the source of the Isere.
FOURNIER, a fief of Lower Canada, in the co. of L'Ilet, fronting the St. Lawrence, and watered by the Bras-St.-Nicolas and some minor streams.
FOURNIGUE, a small rocky island in the Mediterranean, near the coast of France, 26 m. E of Toulon, dep. of Var, and to the N of the Isles d'Or.
FOURNOLS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Saint-Germain-l'Herm, 9 m. WSW of Ambert, on the r. bank of the Dolore. Pop. 2,072.
FOURON-LE-COMTE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege. Pop. 1,439.
FOURON-SAINT-MARTIN, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege. Pop. 1,107. Pop. of village, 556.
FOURON-SAINT-PIERRE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege. Pop. 430.
FOURQUEPIRE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, dep. of Ellezelles. Pop. 208.

FOURQUES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Le-Mas-d'Agénois. Pop. 1,875.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Gard, cant. of Beaucaire, on the r. bank of the Rhône, 19 m. SE of Nismes. Pop. 1,159.

FOURS, or Four, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Nièvre, arrond. of Nevers. The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,631; in 1841, 8,019. The village, called also Sainte-Catherine, is 32 m. E of Nevers, near the l. bank of the Hélène. Pop. 1,266. It has a manufactory of por- most delightful and romantic parts of the co.; but, FOW celain and a fine glass-work. Fairs are held six times a-year.
FOUSAWATA, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Yamato, 48 m. S of Meaco.
FOUSIGAWA, a river of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Sourouga, which descends from Mount Fousi, and, after a course of about 24 m., throws itself into the bay of Totomina.
FOUSIMI, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Yamasiro, to the S of Meaco, of which it may be considered a suburb.

FOUSIYAMA, or Fouzi, a mountain of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Sourouga, 66 m. SW of Yedo. It is supposed to be the loftiest summit of Japan, and is covered with perpetual snow. Smoke, remarkable for its density and odour, is sometimes emitted from its cone.
FOUSSAIS, or Foussay, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Saint-Hilaire-des- Loges, 9 m. NE of Fontenay-le-Comti. Pop. 1,336. It has manufactories of linen.

FOUSSERET (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, arrond. of Muret. The cant. comprises 17 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,218; in 1841, 7,922. The town is 21 m. SW of Muret, near the l. bank of the Louge. Pop. 2,115. It is noted as the birth-place of the Abbé Sicard.

FOUTCHEON, or Fou-tchoung, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, cap. of the prov. of Kafi, 60 m. W of Yedo.—Also a town in the prov. of Sourouga, 70 m. SW of Yedo, on the shore of the bay of Totomina.—Also a town in the prov. of Tsousima.
FOUTSITZ, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Bingo, 105 m. NE of Monko, and 60 m. W of Osaka.
FOUX (CAPE), a headland of the island of Hayti, in the dep. of the Nord, on the NW coast, between the bays of the Môle-Saint-Nicolas and Cheval- Blanc, in N lat. 19° 46′, and W long. 73° 33′.
FOVANT, a parish in Wilts, 7 m. WSW of Wilton. Area 1,990 acres. Pop. 620.

FOVEAUX (STRAIT), a strait of New Zealand, which separates the island of New Leinster from the SW extremity of the island of Tavai-Poenamoo. It bears considerable general resemblance to Bass strait, but its currents are less violent. Its entrances are studded with small islands, and on the N side is a low tongue of land which forms Port-Macquarie. The most dangerous of its passages is between Centre island at its W entrance, and the coast of New Munster. The most formidable wind is the SW, which prevails in the last and two first months of the year.
FOWBERRY, or Fowbury, a township in the p. of Chatton, Northumberland, 3 m. NE of Wooler, on the N bank of the Till.

FOW-CHING, a district of China, in the prov. of Chih-le, div. of Ho-keën-fu. The town which is its capital is on the r. bank of the Tchang-ho, 15 m. NNW of King, and 150 m. S of Peking. It is a very old town, and now much dilapidated. The surrounding country is picturesque and fertile, and is studded with numerous ruins.
FOW-CHOO, a district of China, in the prov. of Sze-chuen, div. and 55 m. E of Chun-ting-fu, at the confluence of the Khian-kiang river with the Yangtse-kiang, in N lat. 29° 45′, and E long. 107° 38′.

FOWEY, a sea-port, borough, and market-town, in Cornwall, 28 m. SSW of Launceston, and 30 m. WNW of Falmouth, on the W bank of the Fowey, where it falls into the English channel.—Area of p., 1,900 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,155; in 1831, 1,767; in 1841, 1,643.—The town is situated in one of the lying in a valley, is not visible till the ferry be approached. The houses are very irregularly built, and extend above a mile along the bank of the river. The fort of St. Catharine, built in the reign of Henry VIII., upon a pile of rocks bounding a creek of the river, with other two forts of more modern erection between it and the town, now defend the entrance to the river. These forts are all so much elevated that no ship could bring her guns to bear upon them. The harbour is spacious and well secured from the destructive effect of storms, by the surrounding hills. It has excellent anchorage; vessels drawing 3 fath. water may enter at the lowest tide, and go into deeper water above. F. was disfranchised by the reform act; previous to which it returned 2 members. The pilchard fishery conducted from this place is extensive, and employs a great number of vessels. When the fish are expected, people called huers are frequently stationed on the rocks, or sailing boats in the offing, to watch the course of the shoals, and give notice to the fishermen. Besides pilchards, there are shipped at this port large quantities of copper-ore; and china-clay and stone, for the potteries.
FOW-LEANG, a town of China, in the prov. of Keang-se, div. of Faou-choo-fu, 50 m. NE of Faouchoo, in N lat. 29° 20', and E long. 116° 59'.

FOWLER, a township of St. Lawrence co., in the state of New York, U.S., 187 m. NNW of Albany. Its surface is rather hilly, and is drained by Oswegatchie river and its tributaries. Its soil consists of a fertile compound of clay and sandy loam. Pop. in 1840, 1,752.—Also a township of Trumbull co., in the state of Ohio, 10 m. N of Warren. Pop. 935.
FOWLER (BAY), an indentation of the S coast of Australia, in Flinder's Land, in S lat. 32°, and E long. 132° 40′, to the E of a headland of the same name.
FOWLER (POINT), a tongue of land on the S coast of Australia, on the W side of the bay of the same name.
FOWLIS (WEST), a parish in Perthshire, 12 m. WSW of Perth. Pop. 1,609.
FOWLUM, a town of Denmark, in Jutland, in the bail. of Aalborg, S of Lögstör.
FOWNHOPE, a parish in Herefordshire, 6½ m. SE of Hereford, on the Wye. Area 3,870 acres. Pop. 1,004.

FOW-NING, a district of China, in the prov. of Keang-soo, div. of Hwae-gan-fu. The town which is its capital is 40 m. NE of Hwae-gan, on the Tchhing-tsu-ho, near its entrance into Lake Sheyang, in N lat. 33° 43', and E long. 119° 51'.

FOW-PING, a district of China, in the prov. of Chih-le, div. of Ching-ting-fu. The town which is its capital is 60 m. NNW of Ching-ting, and 150 m. SW of Peking, on an affluent of the Cha-ho, in N lat. 38° 52′, and E long. 114° 57′.
FOW-PING-HEEN, a district of China, in the prov. of Chih-le, div. of Ching-ting-fu.
FOW-SHAN, a district of China, in the prov. of Shan-se, div. of Ping-yang-fu. The town which is its capital is 25 m. ESE of Ping-yang, on an affluent of the Fuen-ho.
FOX, a township of Clearfield co., in the state of Pennslyvania, U. S., 143 m. NW of Harrisburg. Pop. in 1840, 319.—Also a township of Carroll co., in the state of New York. Pop. 1,491.
FOX (BAY), an indentation of the N coast of the island of Anticosti, enclosed on the N by a cape of the same name, in N lat. 49° 20′, and W long. 62°.
FOX (CAPE), a headland on the SE coast of Russian America, to the SE of the canal of Revilla- Gigedo, in N lat. 54° 45', and W long. 130° 54'.

FOX (Indians), a tribe of Indians who inhabit the banks of the Mississippi, Wisconsin, Turkey, Wapisipinocon, Calfish, and Rock-rivers, in the states of Huron and Missouri, and in the NW part of the state of Illinois. They work the extensive lead-mines on the banks of the Mississippi and Calfish rivers, named from their discoverer the Dubuque mines. The number of the Fox tribe is estimated at about 1,700 individuals. Their chief lives in a village on the banks of the Calfish.

FOX (GREAT RIVER), a river of Lower Canada, in the co. of Gaspe, which runs through the fief Anse de l'Etang into the gulf of the St. Lawrence, between Griffin's Cove and Little Vallée. About 2 m. to the NW is Little Fox river.

FOX (RIVER), a river of the state of Huron, U. S., which issues from Puckawa lake, in N lat. 43° 47', and W long. 88° 38', runs NE to its confluence with Wolf river, then bends E, and expands into Menomonie lake. Issuing thence, it flows by a short outlet into Winnebago lake, through which it passes, and directing its course through Green Township, falls into Green Bay at Fort-Howard, in N lat. 44° 43', and W long. 87° 23'. It has a total course of 300 m., and is navigable for small boats to the Wisconsin portage, a distance of 270 m. The space at this point, between the Fox and Wisconsin rivers, does not exceed 1 m.—Also a river which has its source in the same state, in N lat. 43° 25', runs SW, enters the state of Illinois, and unites with the river of that name at Ottawa.—Also a river of the state of Illinois which runs along the confines of Clay and Lawrence counties, enters Edwards co., and forms one of the head-streams of Little Wabash river.

FOX'S CHANNEL, a strait of British North America, which runs N from the NW extremity of Hudson's strait, and separates Southampton island and Melville's peninsula on the W, from a still unexplored tract of land on the E. On the N it opens by Fury and Hecla state into Boothia gulf, and on the SW it is connected by Frozen strait with that of Sir T. Roe's Welcome.

FOX ISLAND, an island in the estuary of the St. Lawrence, in Miramichi outer bay. It is 33 m. in length, in a SSE direction, narrow, and partially wooded. A channel 11 m. wide separates it from Portage island.
FOX LAKE, a lake in the state of Huron, U. S., in the Winnebagos district, which discharges itself into Rock river in N lat. 43° 8'.

FOXCOTT, a parish of Buckinghamshire, 2 m. NE of Buckingham, and near the Buckingham canal. Area 740 acres. Pop. 119.—Also a parish in Southampton, ecclesiastically a chapelry to the vicarage and 2 m. NW of Andover. Area 620 acres. Pop. 72.
FOXEARTH, a parish in Essex, 3\frac{1}{2} m. NW of Sudbury. Area 1,960 acres. Pop. 474.
FOXEN, a lake of Sweden, in the laen and 60 m. W of Carlstadt, 9 m. in length, and about 3 in breadth.
FOXFORD, a market and post town in the p. of Toomore, co. Mayo, on the r. bank of the river Moy, 7 m. S of Ballina. Pop. in 1831, 1,068; in 1841, 680.
FOXHALL, a parish in Suffolk, 44 m. SE of Ipswich. Area 1,060 acres. Pop. 200.
FOXHOLES, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 10 m. N of Great Driffield. Area 4,150 acres. Pop. 349. It includes the township of Foxholes with Boythorpe. Area 2,610 acres. Pop. 249.
FOXLEY, a parish in Norfolk, 2 m. SSE of Foulsham. Area 1,840 acres. Pop. 293.—Also a parish in Wilts, 3 m. WSW of Malmesbury. Area 850 acres. Pop. 70.
FOXT, a township in the p. of Ipstones, Staffordshire. Pop. 280.

FOXTON, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 7 m. SSW of Cambridge. Area 1,682 acres. Pop. 452.—Also a parish in Leicestershire, 3 m.
